topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You'd Obey an Order to Hurt Someone

scenes.narration: In 1961, an ordinary man sat in a room and pressed a button. He believed it was sending 450 volts into another human being. He did it anyway.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a heavy industrial electrical switch mounted on a metal panel, dramatic side lighting casting deep shadows, no people

scenes.narration: Stanley Milgram wanted to know one thing — how far would normal people go if someone in authority told them to keep going?
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, a locked door with a single bare bulb overhead, long shadow stretching across a cracked floor, empty corridor

scenes.narration: Sixty-five percent of participants delivered the maximum shock. Not monsters. Teachers. Accountants. Fathers. People exactly like you.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a row of identical empty chairs facing a wall, harsh overhead lighting, stark and institutional atmosphere

scenes.narration: Each time someone hesitated, the authority figure said four words — 'Please continue. It's required.' That was enough to restart obedience.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, a single chair under a spotlight in an empty room, deep shadows surrounding it, cold and isolating

scenes.narration: The participants weren't passive. They sweated. They trembled. They begged to stop. But the uniform in the room outweighed their own conscience.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a white lab coat hanging on a hook casting a human-shaped shadow on the wall, eerie and authoritative

scenes.narration: Milgram called it the agentic state — when you hand your moral judgment to someone above you, you stop feeling responsible for outcomes.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, hands passing a folded piece of paper to another set of hands across a bare desk, close cropped shot, heavy shadow

scenes.narration: This isn't ancient history. It's every whistleblower who stayed silent. Every soldier who followed an illegal order. Every employee who looked away.
scenes.image_prompt: Film noir black and white, a single unlit candle on a table surrounded by darkness, one window letting in a thin strip of cold light

scenes.narration: The most dangerous thing about authority isn't the person giving orders. It's how easily we stop asking whether we should be following them at all.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir, a cracked mirror reflecting a distorted empty room, dramatic chiaroscuro lighting, symbolic and unsettling
